{"step_by_step_thinking": "To determine when first permanent molars begin to calcify, we refer to the embryological development of teeth. According to the document titled 'Anatomy, Head and Neck, Teeth -- Embryology' (Source: MEDCORP), odontogenesis of permanent teeth begins at 20 weeks of embryonic development. Since 20 weeks of gestation is approximately 5 months of intrauterine life, this indicates that permanent teeth, including the first permanent molars, begin to form (and thus calcify) around 5 months of intrauterine life. This is earlier than 6 months, but the closest option to this timing is '6 months of intra uterine life' (Option A). Option C (before deciduous incisors) is incorrect because deciduous incisors begin to form around 6-8 weeks of gestation, while permanent teeth begin at 20 weeks, which is later. Option D (about 1 year after birth) is incorrect because calcification begins in utero, not after birth. Option B (at birth) is also incorrect as calcification starts well before birth. Therefore, the most accurate choice based on the developmental timeline is A.", "answer_choice": "A"}
